items.title: Step 1: Install and Run Containers
items.text: Monitoring is a crucial part of DevOps, helping teams detect issues before they become critical. In this guide, we will install Prometheus, Grafana, and Node Exporter using Podman, an alternative to Docker. By the end, you'll have a fully functional monitoring stack with visual dashboards to analyze system metrics.

items.title: What Are Prometheus, Grafana, and Node Exporter?
1. Prometheus: The Time-Series Database
items.text:
Prometheus is an open-source monitoring system that collects and stores metrics as time-series data. It is widely used in DevOps due to its powerful querying language (PromQL) and easy integration with multiple exporters.

📌 Key Features:

  • Time-series data storage
  • Pull-based metric collection
  • Alerting and rule-based evaluations

items.title: 2. Node Exporter: The System Metrics Collector
items.text:
Node Exporter is a lightweight agent that runs on a machine to collect system metrics such as CPU usage, memory utilization, disk I/O, and network statistics.

📌 Common Metrics Collected:

  • CPU Load: node_cpu_seconds_total
  • Memory Usage: node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes
  • Disk Space: node_filesystem_avail_bytes
  • Network Traffic: node_network_receive_bytes_total

items.title: 3. Grafana: The Visualization Tool
items.text:
Grafana is an open-source tool that helps visualize and analyze time-series data. It allows you to create dashboards with real-time graphs, alerts, and reports.

📌 Why Use Grafana?

  • Connects to multiple data sources (Prometheus, MySQL, AWS CloudWatch, etc.)
  • Beautiful and interactive dashboards
  • Custom alerts and notifications

items.title: Step 1: Install and Run Containers
items.text: We will use Podman, a rootless container runtime similar to Docker, to set up our monitoring stack.

items.title: 1.1 Pull and Run a CentOS Container
items.text:
We'll start by running a CentOS container where we will install Node Exporter.

podman run -dit --name centos_container -p 9100:9100 centos:latest

Verify that the container is running:

podman ps

items.title: 1.2 Run Prometheus
items.text:
Now, let's run Prometheus, which will scrape metrics from Node Exporter.

podman run -d --name prometheus -p 9090:9090 quay.io/prometheus/prometheus

Check if Prometheus is running properly:

podman logs prometheus

You can now access Prometheus at: 👉 http://localhost:9090/query

To enter the Prometheus container:

podman exec -it prometheus /bin/sh

items.title: Step 2: Install and Run Node Exporter
items.text:
Log into the CentOS container:

podman exec -it centos_container /bin/bash

Download and extract Node Exporter:

wget https://github.com/prometheus/node_exporter/releases/download/v1.9.0/node_exporter-1.9.0.linux-386.tar.gz
tar -xvf node_exporter-1.9.0.linux-386.tar.gz
cd node_exporter-1.9.0.linux-386
./node_exporter

Now, you can check the collected metrics at: 👉 http://localhost:9100/metrics

items.title: Step 3: Configure Prometheus to Scrape Node Exporter Metrics
items.text:
Modify the Prometheus configuration file (prometheus.yml):

cd /etc/prometheus
vim prometheus.yml

Add the following configuration:

global:
  scrape_interval: 15s # Collect metrics every 15 seconds

scrape_configs:
  - job_name: "prometheus"
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["localhost:9090"]

  - job_name: "centos_node_exporter"
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["localhost:9100"]

Restart Prometheus:

podman restart prometheus

Check if Prometheus is scraping Node Exporter: 👉 http://localhost:9090/targets

items.title: Step 4: Deploy Grafana
items.text:
Run the Grafana container:

podman run -d --name grafana -p 3000:3000 grafana/grafana

Get the IP address of Prometheus:

podman inspect -f '{{ .NetworkSettings.IPAddress }}' prometheus

Access Grafana at: 👉 http://localhost:3000

📌 Default Login:

  • Username: admin
  • Password: admin (change it on first login)

items.title: Step 5: Add Prometheus as a Data Source in Grafana
items.text:
1. Open Grafana (http://localhost:3000).
  2. Go to Configuration > Data Sources.
  3. Click "Add Data Source", select Prometheus.
  4. Set the URL as http://prometheus:9090.
  5. Click "Save & Test".

items.title: Step 6: Import Node Exporter Dashboard
items.text:
1. In Grafana, go to "Create" > "Import".
  2. Enter the Dashboard ID: 1860 (or search for "Node Exporter Full").
  3. Select Prometheus as the data source.
  4. Click "Import".

You should now see a full system monitoring dashboard! 🎉

items.title: Step 7: Managing Containers
items.text:
To stop and remove containers:

podman stop centos_container
podman rm centos_container

Restart Prometheus and Grafana:

podman restart prometheus
podman restart grafana

items.title: Shell Scripts for SRE and DevOps
items.text:
Shell scripts are programs written for command-line interpreters (shells) that automate system tasks and operations. They combine sequences of commands into reusable scripts that can be executed as single units.

They are widely used by SRE (Site Reliability Engineers) and DevOps engineers to automate operations, reduce manual effort, and standardize workflows.
path: Shell Scripts for SRE and DevOps

items.title: Historical Context and Evolution
items.text:
Shell scripting originated in the early Unix systems (1970s) with the Bourne shell (sh). Over time, more feature-rich shells emerged:

  • Bash (Bourne Again Shell): Default on most Linux distributions
  • Zsh: Extended features with better user interaction
  • Ksh: AIX default shell with advanced scripting capabilities
path: Shell Scripts for SRE and DevOps

items.title: Why Shell Scripts Are Important for SRE and DevOps
items.text:
Tip

Shell scripts remain a core tool for SRE and DevOps. - They are simple: use plain Linux commands. - They are powerful: integrate system utilities, APIs, and services. - They are universal: available by default on nearly every Unix/Linux system. - They allow conditions, loops, functions, and modularity to make tasks reusable.

Examples of usage:

  • Daily health checks
  • Disk and memory monitoring
  • Service management
  • Deployment pipelines
  • Cron-based automation
path: Shell Scripts for SRE and DevOps

items.title: Shell Interpreter and Location
items.text:
• The interpreter is the program that executes shell scripts.
  • Common locations:
/bin/sh
/bin/bash
/usr/bin/zsh
  • Default interpreter: /bin/bash in most Linux systems.
  • Speed: Shell scripts are slower than compiled languages (C, Go), but fast enough for automation.

Note

Always define interpreter at the top of scripts with shebang: bash #!/bin/bash

items.title: SonarQube: Code Quality & Security Analysis
items.text:
SonarQube is an open-source platform for continuous inspection of code quality. It automatically detects bugs, vulnerabilities, code smells, and measures technical debt, duplication, and test coverage.

This guide covers:

  • Installing SonarQube on Ubuntu
  • Basic configuration with PostgreSQL
  • Integrating with Jenkins for CI/CD

items.title: Key Benefits
items.text:
• Early detection of issues in the development lifecycle
  • Supports 30+ programming languages
  • Seamless integration with Jenkins, GitHub, GitLab, etc.
  • Quality Gates to enforce code standards

Official Documentation: SonarQube Docs

Download Latest Version: SonarQube Downloads

??? tip "Recommended Hardware" - Minimum: 4 GB RAM (8 GB+ recommended for production) - Java 17 (required for SonarQube Server) - PostgreSQL 12+

items.title: Configure Database Connection
items.text:
Edit the configuration file:

sudo nano /opt/sonarqube/conf/sonar.properties

Add/replace these lines:

sonar.jdbc.username=sonar
sonar.jdbc.password=sonar
sonar.jdbc.url=jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/sonar

items.title: Start SonarQube
items.text:
cd /opt/sonarqube/bin/linux-x86-64
sudo -u sonar ./sonar.sh start

Check status:

sudo -u sonar ./sonar.sh status

Access SonarQube at: http://your-server-ip:9000

Default credentials:

  • Username: admin
  • Password: admin

Tip: For production, set up as a systemd service.

Official Guide: Install the Server

items.title: SonarQube Installation on Ubuntu
items.text: This guide walks you through installing SonarQube on Ubuntu using PostgreSQL.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• Ubuntu 20.04 / 22.04 / 24.04
  • At least 4 GB RAM (8 GB recommended)
  • Java 17
sudo apt update
sudo apt install openjdk-17-jdk -y
java -version

items.title: Step 1: Install PostgreSQL
items.text:
sudo apt install postgresql postgresql-contrib -y

Create database and user:

sudo -u postgres psql

CREATE USER sonar WITH PASSWORD 'sonar';
CREATE DATABASE sonar OWNER sonar;
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE sonar TO sonar;
\q

items.title: Step 2: Download and Install SonarQube
items.text:
Download the latest version from the official downloads page.

# Example for latest version (replace with current link)
wget https://binaries.sonarsource.com/Distribution/sonarqube/sonarqube-2026.1.2.zip

sudo apt install unzip -y
unzip sonarqube-*.zip
sudo mv sonarqube-* /opt/sonarqube

items.title: Step 3: Create Dedicated User (Recommended)
items.text:
sudo useradd -r -m -U -d /opt/sonarqube -s /bin/false sonar
sudo chown -R sonar:sonar /opt/sonarqube

items.title: Step 1: Install SonarQube Scanner Plugin
items.text:
1. Go to Jenkins → Manage Jenkins → Manage Plugins
  2. Search for "SonarQube Scanner" in the Available tab
  3. Install and restart Jenkins

items.title: Step 2: Configure SonarQube Server in Jenkins
items.text:
1. Manage Jenkins → Configure System
  2. Scroll to SonarQube servers section
  3. Click Add SonarQube
  4. Name: SonarQube
  5. Server URL: http://your-sonarqube-server:9000
  6. Server Authentication Token: (Generate from SonarQube → My Account → Security)

Next: Pipeline Example →

Official Jenkins Integration: SonarQube Jenkins Docs

items.title: Example Pipeline (Declarative)
items.text:
pipeline {
    agent any

    environment {
        SONAR_TOKEN = credentials('sonar-token')  // Add your token in Jenkins Credentials
    }

    stages {
        stage('Checkout') {
            steps {
                git branch: 'main', url: 'https://github.com/your-repo.git'
            }
        }

        stage('SonarQube Analysis') {
            steps {
                withSonarQubeEnv('SonarQube') {  // Name must match Jenkins config
                    sh '''
                        sonar-scanner \
                          -Dsonar.projectKey=your-project-key \
                          -Dsonar.sources=. \
                          -Dsonar.host.url=http://your-sonarqube:9000 \
                          -Dsonar.token=$SONAR_TOKEN
                    '''
                }
            }
        }

        stage('Quality Gate') {
            steps {
                timeout(time: 10, unit: 'MINUTES') {
                    waitForQualityGate abortPipeline: true
                }
            }
        }

        stage('Build') {
            steps {
                sh 'mvn clean package'   // or your build command
            }
        }
    }
}

items.title: How to Use
items.text:
1. Create a new Pipeline job in Jenkins
  2. Paste the script or use Jenkinsfile from repository
  3. Add sonar-token as Secret Text credential in Jenkins

Tip: Use waitForQualityGate to fail the build if quality standards are not met.

items.title: Why Terraform
items.text:
Terraform is an Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool by HashiCorp that allows you to define, provision, and manage infrastructure across cloud providers using declarative configuration files.

  • Ideal for creating and managing infrastructure.
  • Uses HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) — a human-readable, declarative language.

items.title: Example
items.text:
resource "aws_instance" "example" {
  ami           = "ami-0abcd1234"
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
}

items.title: How Terraform Communicates with Cloud Providers
items.text:
1. Providers (Plugins) Terraform uses provider plugins to communicate with clouds.

  2. hashicorp/aws for AWS

  3. hashicorp/azurerm for Azure

  4. hashicorp/google for GCP

  5. APIs Each provider calls the official cloud API (REST or SDK) to create resources.

  6. Authentication Terraform needs credentials to access APIs:

provider "aws" {
  access_key = "YOUR_ACCESS_KEY"
  secret_key = "YOUR_SECRET_KEY"
  region     = "us-east-1"
}

  1. Terraform Workflow

  2. init → Download providers

  3. plan → Preview changes
  4. apply → Create or modify resources
  5. state → Save results locally or remotely

items.title: Example Flow
items.text:
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "demo" {
  bucket = "my-demo-bucket"
}

Terraform uses the AWS provider to authenticate, send an API call, and record the result in terraform.tfstate.

Summary: Terraform communicates through provider plugins that use official cloud APIs to create and manage resources.

items.title: 1. Install AWS CLI
items.text:
macOS

brew install awscli
aws --version

Windows

choco install awscli
aws --version

Manual installers are available at: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/getting-started-install.html

aws configure

AWS Access Key ID [None]: test
AWS Secret Access Key [None]: test
Default region name [None]: us-east-1
Default output format [None]: json

Configuration files:

~/.aws/credentials
~/.aws/config

aws s3 ls

With LocalStack:

aws --endpoint-url=http://localhost:4566 s3 ls

aws configure --profile localstack
aws --profile localstack --endpoint-url=http://localhost:4566 s3 ls

items.title: LocalStack Setup
items.text: LocalStack emulates AWS services locally using Docker — perfect for testing Terraform and AWS CLI without real AWS credentials.

items.title: Why Use LocalStack
items.text:
• Avoid AWS costs
  • Work offline
  • Fast and safe testing
  • Supports S3, EC2, Lambda, DynamoDB, and more

items.title: 1. Prerequisites
items.text: Tool Purpose macOS Install Command Docker Desktop Runs containers brew install --cask docker Python 3.8+ Required for CLI Preinstalled pip Python package manager python3 -m ensurepip --upgrade

items.title: 2. Install LocalStack
items.text:
pip install localstack awscli-local
localstack --version

items.title: 3. Start LocalStack
items.text:
localstack start

Runs on http://localhost:4566.

items.title: 4. Web Dashboard
items.text: Terminal

items.title: 5. Test
items.text:
awslocal s3 ls
awslocal s3 mb s3://demo-bucket

items.title: 6. Docker Compose Example
items.text:
version: "3.8"
services:
  localstack:
    image: localstack/localstack
    ports:
      - "4566:4566"
    environment:
      - SERVICES=s3,ec2,lambda
      - DEBUG=1
    volumes:
      - "./localstack:/var/lib/localstack"

Run:

docker-compose up

items.text:

├── main.tf
├── provider.tf
├── outputs.tf

items.title: Step 1: Create a Test File
items.text: echo "Hello from Terraform + LocalStack!" > hello.txt

items.title: Step 2: provider.tf
items.text:
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"
}

provider "aws" {
  region                      = "us-east-1"
  access_key                  = "test"
  secret_key                  = "test"
  s3_force_path_style         = true
  skip_credentials_validation = true
  skip_metadata_api_check     = true
  skip_requesting_account_id  = true
  endpoints {
    s3 = "http://localhost:4566"
  }
}

items.title: Step 3: main.tf
items.text:
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "demo" {
  bucket = "terraform-localstack-demo"
}

resource "aws_s3_object" "file_upload" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.demo.id
  key    = "hello.txt"
  source = "hello.txt"
  etag   = filemd5("hello.txt")
}

items.title: Step 4: outputs.tf
items.text:
output "bucket_name" {
  value = aws_s3_bucket.demo.bucket
}

items.title: Step 5: Deploy
items.text:
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply -auto-approve

Verify:

awslocal s3 ls
awslocal s3 ls s3://terraform-localstack-demo/

items.title: Destroy Resources
items.text:
terraform destroy -auto-approve

Output:

Destroy complete! Resources: 2 destroyed.

items.title: Optional: Preview Destroy
items.text: terraform plan -destroy

items.title: Verify Deletion
items.text: awslocal s3 ls

items.title: Reset LocalStack (Optional)
items.text:
localstack stop
docker rm -f localstack
docker volume prune -f
localstack start
